cards.front: What does each section of OCR J351 Component 01 require?
cards.back: Section A: read and respond to unseen non-fiction, including one 19th-century text. Section B: produce a piece of non-fiction writing.
cards.explanation: Component 01 separates reading and writing: Section A uses unseen non-fiction and includes a 19th-century text; Section B requires non-fiction writing.
cards.memoryTip: Pair A with “read” and B with “write”; pin the 19th-century text to A.

choices.text: Section A: produce a piece of non-fiction writing. Section B: read and respond to unseen non-fiction, including one 19th-century text.
choices.feedback: The section roles are reversed: Section A is reading and Section B is writing.

choices.text: Section A: read and respond to unseen non-fiction, including one 19th-century text. Section B: produce a piece of non-fiction writing.
choices.feedback: Correct: Section A covers unseen non-fiction reading, while Section B covers non-fiction writing.

choices.text: Section A: read unseen prose fiction from the 20th or 21st century. Section B: produce original creative writing.
choices.feedback: Component 01 specifies unseen non-fiction reading and non-fiction writing.

choices.text: Section A: read a prepared non-fiction text. Section B: give a spoken presentation.
choices.feedback: Section A uses unseen texts, and Section B requires a piece of non-fiction writing.

cards.subject: English Language

sources.title: Cambridge OCR GCSE English Language (J351)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 10-13; 01 Communicating information and ideas
sources.excerpt:
For Section A, learners read and respond to unseen
non-fiction texts, one of which is a 19th century text.
For Section B, they produce a piece of non-fiction
writing.

cards.front: In Component 01 reading, what should a learner do with ideas from one text versus ideas from more than one text?
cards.back: Summarise ideas and information from a single text; synthesise from more than one text.
cards.explanation: The required operation changes with the number of texts: one text is summarised, while material from more than one text is synthesised.
cards.memoryTip: Count before acting: 1 text = summarise; 2 or more = synthesise.

choices.text: Synthesise ideas from a single text; summarise ideas from more than one text.
choices.feedback: This reverses the requirement: synthesis applies to more than one text.

choices.text: Summarise each text separately and stop there.
choices.feedback: With more than one text, the requirement includes synthesis rather than separate summaries alone.

choices.text: Identify ideas without summarising or synthesising them.
choices.feedback: Identification alone omits the specified summary and synthesis operations.

choices.text: Summarise ideas and information from a single text; synthesise from more than one text.
choices.feedback: Correct: summary applies to one text and synthesis to multiple texts.

cards.subject: English Language

sources.title: Cambridge OCR GCSE English Language (J351)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 10-13; 01.A Reading information and ideas
sources.excerpt:
• summarise ideas and information from a
single text and synthesise from more than
one text

cards.front: How should a learner evaluate the usefulness of a text in Component 01?
cards.back: Identify bias and misuse of evidence.
cards.explanation: Usefulness is evaluated through two checks: whether the text shows bias and whether it misuses evidence.
cards.memoryTip: Use the B–E check: Bias and Evidence misuse.

choices.text: Identify bias and misuse of evidence.
choices.feedback: Correct: these are the two specified checks for usefulness.

choices.text: Identify only the text’s main ideas.
choices.feedback: Main ideas alone do not address the specified checks of bias and evidence misuse.

choices.text: Summarise the text without checking its evidence.
choices.feedback: Summary does not replace evaluation of bias and misuse of evidence.

choices.text: List vocabulary and grammatical features only.
choices.feedback: Feature-spotting alone does not perform the specified usefulness check.

cards.subject: English Language

sources.title: Cambridge OCR GCSE English Language (J351)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 10-13; 01.A Reading information and ideas
sources.excerpt:
• evaluate the usefulness of a text by
identifying bias and misuse of evidence

cards.front: Which three features should be adapted as appropriate in Component 01 non-fiction writing?
cards.explanation: The specification names tone, style and register as the three features to adapt appropriately.
cards.memoryTip: Recall the source order with TSR: tone, style, register.

choices.text: Audience, purpose and context.
choices.feedback: These guide writing decisions, but they are not the three features named in this adaptation requirement.

choices.text: Vocabulary, form and grammatical features.
choices.feedback: This is not the specified trio; the requirement names tone, style and register.

choices.text: Tone, style and register.
choices.feedback: Correct: all three should be adapted as appropriate.

choices.text: Sentence structures, punctuation and spelling.
choices.feedback: These relate to range and accuracy, not the explicitly named adaptation trio.

cards.subject: English Language

sources.title: Cambridge OCR GCSE English Language (J351)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 10-13; 01.B Writing for audience, impact and purpose
sources.excerpt:
• adapt tone, style and register as
appropriate

cards.front: What are the period and genre requirements for the reading texts in Component 02?
cards.back: Both are 20th- or 21st-century literary prose; one may be literary non-fiction, and there is no 19th-century text.
cards.explanation: Both texts fall within 20th- or 21st-century literary prose. One can be literary non-fiction, and a 19th-century text is explicitly excluded.
cards.memoryTip: Use the number route: Component 02 has two texts from the 20th or 21st century, never the 19th; one may be literary non-fiction.

choices.text: One is 19th-century non-fiction and one is 20th- or 21st-century literary prose.
choices.feedback: Component 02 explicitly excludes a 19th-century text.

choices.text: Both are 20th- or 21st-century literary prose; one may be literary non-fiction, and there is no 19th-century text.
choices.feedback: Correct: both texts are literary prose from the 20th or 21st century, with one possible literary non-fiction text.

choices.text: Both are 19th-century non-fiction texts.
choices.feedback: The texts are from the 20th or 21st century, not the 19th century.

choices.text: Both are 20th- or 21st-century drama or poetry texts.
choices.feedback: The specified genre is literary prose.

cards.subject: English Language

sources.title: Cambridge OCR GCSE English Language (J351)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 10-15; 02 Exploring effects and impact
sources.excerpt:
• Learners read and respond to
literary prose texts, one text may be
literary non-fiction.
• Both texts are 20th or 21st century
literary prose. There will not be a
19th century text in component 02.

cards.front: Which three aspects of writers’ craft should learners explore for their impact on the reader in Component 02?
cards.explanation: Reader impact is explored through writers’ use of all three named aspects: language, structure and grammatical features.
cards.memoryTip: Follow L–S–G in order: language, structure, grammatical features.

choices.text: Vocabulary, form and audience.
choices.feedback: Audience is not part of the named reader-impact trio.

choices.text: Bias, misuse of evidence and context.
choices.feedback: These are not the three aspects named for exploring impact on the reader.

choices.text: Tone, style and register.
choices.feedback: This is not the trio specified for exploring reader impact in the excerpt.

choices.text: Language, structure and grammatical features.
choices.feedback: Correct: learners explore how writers’ use of these three aspects affects the reader.

cards.subject: English Language

sources.title: Cambridge OCR GCSE English Language (J351)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 10-15; 02.A Reading meaning and effects
sources.excerpt:
They explore the impact of writers’
uses of language, structure and
grammatical features on the reader.

cards.front: In Component 02 narrative writing, what should organising and structuring ideas create?
cards.explanation: Organisation and structure in narrative writing are used to create effects deliberately.
cards.memoryTip: Turn the instruction into an arrow: organise + structure → deliberate effects.

choices.text: Deliberate effects.
choices.feedback: Correct: deliberate effects are the specified aim of organisation and structure.

choices.text: A single prescribed form.
choices.feedback: The cited requirement links organisation and structure to deliberate effects, not a prescribed form.

choices.text: A summary of the source texts.
choices.feedback: The requirement concerns creating deliberate effects in narrative writing, not summarising texts.

choices.text: A list of ideas without structure.
choices.feedback: This removes the organisation and structure required to create deliberate effects.

cards.subject: English Language

sources.title: Cambridge OCR GCSE English Language (J351)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 10-15; 02.B Writing imaginatively and creatively
sources.excerpt:
• organise and structure ideas in narrative
writing to create deliberate effects

cards.front: What control of viewpoint is required across a Component 02 creative-writing piece?
cards.back: Maintain a consistent viewpoint and make conscious decisions about narrative point of view.
cards.explanation: Consistency must continue across the piece, and narrative point of view should be a conscious decision.
cards.memoryTip: Choose the narrative lens consciously, then keep that lens consistent across the piece.

choices.text: Change viewpoint regularly to create variety.
choices.feedback: Regular changes conflict with the requirement to maintain a consistent viewpoint.

choices.text: Avoid making decisions about narrative point of view.
choices.feedback: The specification requires conscious decisions about narrative point of view.

choices.text: Maintain a consistent viewpoint and make conscious decisions about narrative point of view.
choices.feedback: Correct: viewpoint should be consciously controlled and consistent across the piece.

choices.text: Keep the opening viewpoint only; later viewpoint choices do not matter.
choices.feedback: Consistency is required across the whole piece, not only at the opening.

cards.subject: English Language

sources.title: Cambridge OCR GCSE English Language (J351)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 10-15; 02.B Writing imaginatively and creatively
sources.excerpt:
• maintain a consistent viewpoint across
a piece of writing, making conscious
decisions, for example, about narrative
point of view
